coredata.dc:title: Chemical-tongue sensor array for determination of multiple metal ions based on trichromatic lanthanide-based nanomaterials
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xiao
Wang, Tianlin
Zhao, Minyang
Wang, Pengxiang
Hao, Zhe
Lin, Xiaodong
Wang, Shuo
Liu, Yaqing
coredata.dc:description: Rapid detection and identification of multiple metal ions is significant in that toxic metal ions are harmful to public health and environmental safety. Herein, a fluorescent sensor array composed of three lanthanide-based fluorescent probes (Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP, and Eu-BOP) was developed as a novel tongue-mimic chemosensor array for rapid discrimination of multiple metal ions. The three fluorescent probes exhibit different binding affinities toward metal ions, resulting in diverse fluorescence behaviors and distinct fluorescence response patterns. The patterns can act as “fingerprints” for identification of multiple metal ions via principal component analysis (PCA) technology. The Ce-ATP/Tb-GMP/Eu-BOP sensor array achieved good discrimination of eleven kinds of metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cr6+, Mn7+). It is still sensitive in mixtures of multiple metal ions and would be potentially fit for analysis of complex composition. Interestingly, the sensor array can discriminate metal ions with different valence, for example Cr3+ and Cr6+. With tap water as real sample, the eleven metal ions can be well distinguished with excellent recovery, indicating promising potential application in evaluating water quality and environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence sensor array
Principal component analysis
Multiple metal ions discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent sensor arrays for metal ions sensing
The possibility for differentiating multiple metal ions
Discrimination of metal ions in tap water samples

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775108 ) and Construction of Platform for State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety ( 18PTSYJC00130 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra (A) and CIE chromaticity diagram (B) of Ce-ATP (a), Tb-GMP (b), Eu-BOP (c). The fluorescence responses of the Ce-ATP (C), Tb-GMP (D) and Eu-BOP (E) against 11 metal ions (Mn7+ (1), Cr3+ (2), Cu2+ (3), Pb2+ (4), Ag+ (5), Cr6+ (6), Fe3+ (7), Co2+ (8), Mn2+ (9), Cd2+ (10), Zn2+ (11)).

Fluorescence intensity of the Ce-ATP (at 360 nm), Tb-GMP (at 544 nm) and Eu-BOP (at 614 nm) sensor toward the 11 metal ions (200 μM) (A). PCA plot (B), HCA plot (C) and heat mapping (D) of the sensor array against the 11 metal ions. The error bars represented the calculated standard deviation based on five independent measurements.

Rader plots of the sensor array against Cu2+ (A), Cr3+ (B), and Fe3+(C) with different concentrations. PCA plots of the sensor array against Cu2+ (A1); Cr3+ (B1), and Fe3+(C1) with different concentrations. Plots of PC1 as a function of Cu2+ (0.1-40 μM, A2), Cr3+ (0.5-30 μM, B2), and Fe3+ (5-70 μM, C2) with different concentrations. The error bars were obtained based on five independent measurements.

Discrimination of metal ion mixtures with different concentrations with the Ce-ATP/Tb-GMP/Eu-BOP sensor array. Rader plots (A-C), PCA plots (A1-C1), PC1 plots (A2-C2) of the sensor array toward the mixtures with different concentrations: (A, A1, A2) for Cu2+-Cd2+ (0:5, 0.1:4.9, 0.2:4.8, 0.5:4.5, 1:4, 2:3, 3:2, 4:1 and 5:0, all for μM), (B, B1, B2) for Cu2+-Cd2+-Fe3+ (0.1:4.9:10, 0.2:4.8:10, 0.5:4.5:10, 1:4:10, 2:3:10, 3:2:10 and 4:1:10, all for μM), (C, C1, C2) for Cr3+-Cr6+ (0:10, 0.5:9.5, 1:9, 2:8, 3:7, 5:5, 7:3, 9:1 and 10:0, all for μM).
PCA plot (A) and HCA plot (B) of the sensor array against the 11 metal ions (200 μM) in tap water samples.
Schematic illustration of the construction of Ce-ATP/Tb-GMP/Eu-BOP sensor array (a) and discrimination of multiple metal ions via PCA technology (b).
Detection of Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ in tap water using the sensor array.

head.ce:title: Chemical-tongue sensor array for determination of multiple metal ions based on trichromatic lanthanide-based nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, College of Food Engineering and Biotechnology, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid detection and identification of multiple metal ions is significant in that toxic metal ions are harmful to public health and environmental safety. Herein, a fluorescent sensor array composed of three lanthanide-based fluorescent probes (Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP, and Eu-BOP) was developed as a novel tongue-mimic chemosensor array for rapid discrimination of multiple metal ions. The three fluorescent probes exhibit different binding affinities toward metal ions, resulting in diverse fluorescence behaviors and distinct fluorescence response patterns. The patterns can act as “fingerprints” for identification of multiple metal ions via principal component analysis (PCA) technology. The Ce-ATP/Tb-GMP/Eu-BOP sensor array achieved good discrimination of eleven kinds of metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cr6+, Mn7+). It is still sensitive in mixtures of multiple metal ions and would be potentially fit for analysis of complex composition. Interestingly, the sensor array can discriminate metal ions with different valence, for example Cr3+ and Cr6+. With tap water as real sample, the eleven metal ions can be well distinguished with excellent recovery, indicating promising potential application in evaluating water quality and environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic metal ions at even very low concentrations can cause serious environmental and health problems [1–3]. Humans and other living species can be exposed to toxic metal ions through water, air, soil, and food [4–6]. The harmful impacts of metal ions have drawn great attention on developing biosensors for rapid discrimination and detection of multiple metal ions. Currently, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) are widely used as classic standard methods for quantitative detection metal ions [7,8]. Although such technologies display high specificity and sensitivity, they suffer limitations from high cost, complicated procedure and expensive instruments requirement. Fluorescence assays have aroused considerable attention due to the advantages of sensitivity, simplicity, convenience and possibility for on-site detection [9,10]. It is worthy of noting that traditional detection methods based on “lock-and-key” demand highly specific recognition receptor for single metal ion, which cannot simultaneously detect multiple metal ions [11–13]. Sensor array can act as chemical tongue to provide promising and effective approach to solve the issue. A sensor array is composed of a series of sensor elements, which generates unique response to each of targets [14]. Different responses can be quantified and identified as unique fingerprints through principal component analysis (PCA), thus enabling the simultaneous differentiation of multiple targets [15,16]. Various fluorescent sensor arrays have been constructed for the detection of multiple metal ions with semiconductor quantum dots, organic fluorescence dyes and fluorescence nanoparticles as probes. Cheng's group constructed a fluorescent sensor array system to distinguish four kinds of metal ions with four quantum dots as probes [17]. Kool’s group developed a fluorescent sensor array containing six organic fluorescent dyes for detection of multiple metal ions [18]. Although the previous investigations have reached impressive achievement, there is still a great challenge to develop sensor arrays as chemical tongue in low cost, high efficiency and high repeatability with simple operation.

In recently, lanthanide-based fluorescence sensors have attracted increasing interesting due to their unique characteristics such as long fluorescence life, large Stokes displacement and sharp emission bands [19–21]. We herein constructed a novel fluorescent sensor array consisted of three lanthanide-based fluorescent probes for identification of eleven metal ions. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the coordination of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and Ce3+ ions form Ce-ATP probe with blue emission. Guanosine monophosphate (GMP) coordinates with Tb3+ ions to form Tb-GMP probe with green emission. 5-boronobenzene-1, 3-dicarboxylic acid (BOP) coordinates with Eu3+ to form Eu-BOP probe with red emission. Since different metal ions have different affinity to the sensor probes, the changes of fluorescence intensity caused by each metal ion result in a fluorescence pattern which is further used for differentiating metal ions via PCA. As a multivariate discrimination method, PCA can transform the original data set into linearly uncorrelated principal components (PC) through matrix translation to achieve dimensionality reduction of the data [22–25]. Here, metal ions including Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cr6+ and Mn7+ can be well identified and classified. In addition, quantitative detection of metal ions and discrimination of metal ions in the real sample of tap water was also demonstrated.

FeCl3, CuCl2, CrCl3, CoCl2, ZnCl2, CdCl2, AgNO3, Pb(CH3COO)2, Mn(CH3COO)2, K2Cr2O7, KMnO4 were reagent grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai). Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, Tb(NO3)3·6H2O, EuCl3·6H2O, tris(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris), and 5′-monophosphate disodium salt hydrate (GMP) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) was purchased from Solarbio Reagent Co. Ltd. (Beijing). 3,5-dicarboxybenzeneboronic acid (BOP) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai).

Fluorescence spectras were recorded with a LUMINA Fluorescence Spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were measured with the ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo, USA). The morphology of Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP, Eu-BOP was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI TALOS). The contents of Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ in tap water were analyzed by NexION 350X ICP-MS (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent sensor arrays for metal ions sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP and Eu-BOP were synthesized with slight change according to literature procedures [26–28], which were characterized via TEM and XPS (See Figs. S1 and S2, respectively, in supporting information (SI)). The following procedure was used to detect metal ions: HEPES buffer (N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N-2′-ethanesulfonic acid, 10 mM, pH 7.4, 340 μL) were respectively added into three tubes. Then, Ce-ATP (40 μL, 1.0 mM), Tb-GMP (40 μL, 1.0 mM), and Eu-BOP (40 μL, 2.0 mg/mL) were respectively added into tube to form three different sensors. After incubation for 2 min at room temperature, metal ions (20 μL) with different concentrations were added into each tube. After incubating for 5 min, the fluorescence responses of the sensor array of Ce- ATP, Tb-GMP and Eu-BOP were recorded. Eleven metal ions were respectively reacted with three probes, which was repeated five times. PCA was applied to the obtained training data matrix (3 probes × 11 metal ions × 5 replicates) for further analysis and processing.
Origin 2019b software was used to perform principal component analysis (PCA), hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) and heat mapping. Each target was repeated in five replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possibility for differentiating multiple metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in the fluorescence spectra, Fig. 1 A, the maximum emission peaks were found at 360 nm for Ce-ATP (curve a), 544 nm for Tb-GMP (curve b) and 614 nm for Eu-BOP (curve c), which are corresponding to the CIE chromaticity diagram at (0.157, 0.155), (0.224, 0.627) and (0.567, 0.334) respectively, Fig. 1B. It was found that pH 7.4 was the optimal recognition performance for the sensor array (See Fig. S3A-C in SI), which was selected for the following measurement for distinguishing eleven metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cr6+, Mn7+). Cu2+ was used as an example to further explore the optimal incubation time of metal ions in the sensing system (See Fig. S4 in SI). The fluorescence response reached balance after incubation for five minutes, which was used in the following experiments.

For the fluorescence probe of Ce-ATP with blue emission, metal ions can bind with N7 site of ATP to form a closed structure, which results in different fluorescence changes when different metal ions were incubated with the Ce-ATP sensing system, Fig. 1C [29]. For the fluorescence probe of Tb-GMP with green emission, the abundant functional groups in GMP provide coordination cites for metal ions [30]. Since different binding affinities, the Tb-GMP exhibits different fluorescence responses after reacting with the eleven metal ions, Fig. 1D. As for Eu-BOP with red emission, the functional groups of BOP could be complexed with some metal ions resulting in the fluorescence changes of Eu-BOP with different degrees, Fig. 1E [31]. In general, upon challenged with eleven metal ions (Cu2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ag+, Cr6+, Mn7+), the three probes exhibit diverse fluorescence changes and respond differently to the metal ions on account of the different coordination affinities between the metal ions and the fluorescent probes. The fluorescent responses’ combination of the three probes towards each metal ion would generate a unique pattern for differentiating the multiple metal ions via PCA.

Based on the distinct fluorescence responses of Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP and Eu-BOP to the eleven metal ions, the sensor array (Ce-ATP/Tb-GMP/Eu-BOP) was constructed by integrating the three fluorescence sensing probes for pattern recognition of multiple metal ions. Here, the emission peaks at 360 nm of Ce-ATP, 544 nm of Tb-GMP and 614 nm of Eu-BOP were taken as the output signals. According to the distinct fluorescence signals change (F/F0) of sensors induced by multiple metal ions, Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence response patterns of the training matrix (3 probes × 11 metal ions × 5 replicates) were statistically analyzed with PCA. Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence probe in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. Principle components (PC1 and PC2) derived from the PCA processing were plotted in Fig. 2B. The data points of each metal ion from five repeated trials form tight clusters with a substantial separation from those of the other metal ions. The first PC (PC1) accounts for 69.1 % and the second component (PC2) contributes 27.3 % of the variability in the data, which contributes 96.4 % of the total variance, confirming the reliability of the developed sensing array for effective distinguishing the eleven metal ions. More interestingly, the sensor array can also distinguish metal ion with different valence states, such as Cr3+ from Cr6+. As well known that Cr6+ is serious toxic to human body. Cr3+ is essential element for human while excess Cr3+ is harmful to the human health. It is critical to distinguish the chromium with different valence states which is rarely reached in previous reports. Here, the possible mechanism for distinguishing Cr3+ from Cr6+ might be ascribed to the different overlap of the absorption of analyte with the excitation and/or emission of the fluorescence probe which causes different intermolecular energy transfer between them (Fig. S5 in SI) [32,33]. To further analyze the fluorescent fingerprints more clearly, hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) was employed as a statistical technique to classify multiple targets based on Euclidean distance and the output signals which have similar magnitude [34–36]. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence response of the proposed sensor array was clearly divided into four categories, which are category of Cr3+, Cr6+, Cu2+ and Mn7+, category of Fe3+, Co2+and Mn2+, category of Cd2+ and Pb2+ and category of Ag+ and Zn2+. The heat mapping further shows unique pattern for each metal ion where the higher color key means high fluorescence intensity, whereby a smaller F corresponds to lower color key. It confirms that the eleven metal ions can be well differentiated by the as-constructed sensor array, Fig. 2D. The results reveal that the developed sensor array has excellent differentiability and can exert distinct fluorescence response fingerprints for the individual metal ions.

Cu2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+ ions were used as samples to evaluate the sensitivity of the sensor array. The fluorescence response of the sensor array was quenched in different degrees due to the concentration variation of the Cu2+, Cr3+, and Fe3+, which can be learned from the Rader plots (Fig. 3 A-C) and was further validated via heat mapping (See Fig. S6 in SI). As illustrated in Fig. 3A1-C1, the PCA plots originated from metal ions with different concentrations in five repeated measurements were assigned to certain patterns, which are separated from each other. Here, the PC1 accounts for total date over than 90.0 %, and thus was employed for the quantitative detection of the metal ions. The PC1 scores linearly respond to the concentrations of Cu2+ from 0.1 μM to 40 μM with R2 of 0.993 (Fig. 3A2). Cr3+and Fe3+ was distinguished within concentrations range from 0.5 μM to 30 μM with R2 of 0.990 (Fig. 3B2) and from 5 μM to 70 μM with R2 of 0.999, respectively (Fig. 3C2). The results confirmed that the constructed sensor array was homogeneous and stable and could be utilized for sensitively quantitative detection of metal ions.

The capability of the sensor array for identification metal ions in mixtures was further explored. Diverse mixtures of Cu2+ and Cd2+ (Cu2+-Cd2+), Cu2+, Cd2+ and Fe3+(Cu2+-Cd2+-Fe3+), Cr3+ and Cr6+ (Cr3+-Cr6+) with different concentration ratio were prepared as samples. The Rader plots (Fig. 4 A-C) and heat mapping results (Fig. S7A-C, in SI) exhibit that each probe has different response to the metal ions. The metal ions in the mixtures with different concentration ratios can be fully discriminated and separated into different clusters with no overlap which are located with the order of molar ratios in PCA plots (Fig. 4A1, B1, C1). The relevant PC1 was used to locate metal ions in the mixtures to determine each one of them (Fig. 4A2, B2, C2). The results demonstrated the excellent and stable discrimination ability of the sensor array.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of metal ions in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Discrimination multiple metal ions in the real sample is challenging to a sensor array. Here, tap water was selected to explore the applicability of the as-developed sensor array in real samples. As shown in Fig. S8 in SI, the tap water has no effect on the three probes of the sensor array indicating that the constructed sensor array has certain anti-interference capability. The metal ions were spiked into tap water and then incubated with the sensor array. The heat mapping showed different fluorescence response of the sensor array (See Fig. S9 in SI). As illustrated in the PCA plot, Fig. 5 A, the eleven metal ions are sorted into different clusters which are separated from each other, indicating that the metal ions are well distinguished. With the further hierarchical clustering analysis, Fig. 5B, all 55 samples (11 metal ions × 5 replicates) were properly assigned to the corresponding groups with no misclassification, validating the excellent reproducibility of the constructed sensor array for multiple metal ions discrimination. The detection results were comparable with those measured by the traditional ICP-MS method, Table 1 . Meanwhile, nice recoveries were found from 96.8% to 111.9%. The results confirmed that the constructed sensor array had potential applications for metal ions detection in complex sample with robust discrimination ability.

In summary, a fluorescence sensor array has been successfully constructed for detection and discrimination of eleven metal ions with Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP and Eu-BOP as fluorescent probes. Unique fluorescent response pattern is generated according to the different affinity between metal ions and fluorescent probes, which can be analyzed via pattern analysis techniques such as PCA and HCA. Eleven metal ions can be efficiently and quickly detected and distinguished. Interestingly, the sensor array also has capability to distinguish metal ions in binary and ternary mixtures. Specially, metal ions with different valence states can be efficiently distinguished. The excellent recovery for metal ion detection in real sample of tape water validates that the sensor array has potential application in practical application. The developed sensor array system has advantages of low cost, simple operation and high distinguish capability. It is believed that the investigation can broaden the application scope of lanthanide-based sensor array and improve development of chemical tongue sensing platform.

Xiao Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Tianlin Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Minyang Zhao: Formal analysis. Pengxiang Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhe Hao: Investigation. Xiaodong Lin: Formal analysis. Shuo Wang: Resources, Supervision. Yaqing Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775108) and Construction of Platform for State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety (18PTSYJC00130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130107.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of heavy metal ions from wastewaters: a review
J. Environ. Manage.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.L. Fu, Q. Wang, Removal of heavy metal ions from wastewaters: a review. J. Environ. Manage. 92 (2011) 407-418.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent trends in macro-, micro-, and nanomaterial-based tools and strategies for heavy-metal detection
Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Aragay, J. Pons, A. Merkoci, Recent trends in macro-, micro-, and nanomaterial-based tools and strategies for heavy-metal detection. Chem. Rev. 111 (2011) 3433-3458.

Chem. Eng. J.

N. Engl. J. Med.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.W. Clarkson, L. Magos, G.J. Myers, The toxicology of mercury-current exposures and clinical manifestations. N. Engl. J. Med. 349 (2003) 1731-1737.

Lab Chip

ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

Talanta 121 (2014) 127-135.

Sens. Actuators B

Biosens. Bioelectron.

Sens. Actuators B

Sens. Actuators B

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiao Zhou graduated from Bohai University in 2018. Now, she is a master student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research interests mainly include the design and synthesis lanthanide nanomaterials to detect heavy metal ions in food and environment.

Tianlin Wang received his Ph.D. from Tianjin University of Science and Technology in 2020. Currently, his research is focused on intelligent biosensor based on nanomaterials and nanotechnology for food security and serious disease biomarker.

Minyang Zhao obtained his undergraduate degree at Bohai University (2017). He is a Ph.D student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, his research is focused on developing fluorescent probe to detect pathogens in food and for biologic imaging.

Pengxiang Wang graduated from Tianjin University of Science and Technology (2017). He is a master student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, his research is focused on the detection of water in organics and pesticides in food samples based on the Ln-based fluorescent nanoprobes.

Zhe Hao received her undergraduate degree at Tianjin University of Science and Technology (2018). She is a master student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, her research is focusing on portable biosensor with nanomaterials for bioanalytical and food security.

Xiaodong Lin obtained his master degree at Tianjin University of Science and Technology (2017). He is a doctoral student supervised by Prof. Yaqing Liu at State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Currently, his research is focusing on construction of intelligent biosensor and bioimaging with novel nanomaterials.

Professor Shuo Wang received his BS from Nankai University in 1991 and obtained his Ph. D from University of Sydney in 1999. He was a research fellow at University of Sydney and CSIRO Plant Industry from 2001 to 2003. He has been a professor at Tianjin University of Science and Technology since 2003 and currently works in Nankai University. His research is mainly focused on the fundamental theories and applied technology on rapid analysis of trace-level hazardous chemicals in food, and has made a number of pioneering achievements in related academic research frontiers, including the immunoassay for micromolecule, the enrichment and separation technology of trace-level contaminants in food, the synthesis and reaction mechanism of “biomimetic antibody” and the development on the immunoassay theory and technology of substitutable biological antibody. Prof. Wang has over 150 SCI publications. He is awarded Yangtze River Scholars Distinguished Professor, distinguished Young Scholar of the National Science Foundation of China.

Professor Yaqing Liu Prof. Yaqing Liu received her BS from Chengdu University of Sciences and Technology in 1998 and obtained Ph. D from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2006. From 2007 to 2010, Dr. Liu worked as a postdoctoral fellow in Peter Grünberg Institute (PGI-8), Forschungszentrum Jülich, Germany. Then she joined Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry as associate professor in 2010 and became a full professor in 2012. Currently, Prof. Liu works in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research fields involve in synthesis of bio-nanomaterials, fabrication of molecular devices and molecular logic gates, which are further applied in bioanalytical and food security fields. The researches aim to develop novel approaches and technologies for intelligent rapid and accurate detection with high throughput, sensitivity and selectivity. Prof. Liu has over 50 publications, including Angew. Chem. Int. Ed., J. Am. Chem. Soc., ACS Nano, Chem. Sci., NPG Asia. Mater., Nanoscale, Anal. Chem., Biosens. Bioelectron., Chem. Commun. etc
